Name: InfoTheoreticBoundsForLLMCompression
Title: Information-Theoretic Bounds for Efficient Compression of Large Language Models
Short Hypothesis: We hypothesize that by deriving tighter information-theoretic bounds for compressing large language models (LLMs), we can achieve more efficient and accurate compression schemes that outperform existing methods. Specifically, we aim to establish the fundamental bit-cost of preserving predictive sufficiency in LLMs and develop practical compression algorithms that approach these theoretical limits.
Related Work: Existing work in model compression has primarily focused on empirical methods and heuristics, often lacking a rigorous information-theoretic foundation. Recent works like EMQ [1], AMP-ViT [2], and SNIP [3] have made significant strides in mixed-precision quantization and adaptive precision training, but they do not provide a theoretical framework for understanding the fundamental limits of compression. Our work aims to fill this gap by deriving achievable and converse bounds for LLM compression, leveraging rate-distortion theory and information-theoretic principles.
Abstract: We propose a novel approach to the compression of large language models (LLMs) by deriving information-theoretic bounds that quantify the fundamental bit-cost of preserving predictive sufficiency. Our method leverages rate-distortion theory to establish achievable and converse bounds, providing a theoretical foundation for understanding the limits of LLM compression. We develop practical compression algorithms that approach these bounds, using entropy estimators and mutual information lower bounds to allocate bits optimally. Our experiments on state-of-the-art LLMs demonstrate that our approach outperforms existing methods in terms of both compression efficiency and model accuracy, while maintaining theoretical guarantees.
Experiments: Derive the rate-distortion function for LLMs, considering both weight and activation compression.
Develop an entropy estimator for LLM parameters and activations to quantify the information content.
Implement a compression algorithm that uses mutual information lower bounds to allocate bits optimally across layers and parameters.
Evaluate the performance of the compression algorithm on a variety of LLMs, including BERT, GPT, and T5, using standard benchmarks such as GLUE and SuperGLUE.
Compare the compression efficiency and model accuracy of our method against existing state-of-the-art techniques, including EMQ, AMP-ViT, and SNIP.
Analyze the theoretical bounds and experimental results to validate the hypothesis and identify potential areas for further improvement.
Risk Factors and Limitations: The theoretical bounds may be difficult to derive for complex LLM architectures, and approximations may be necessary.
The practical implementation of the compression algorithm may face challenges in terms of computational efficiency and scalability.
The entropy and mutual information estimators may introduce biases or inaccuracies, affecting the performance of the compression algorithm.
The proposed method may not generalize well to all types of LLMs or datasets, and additional tuning may be required for optimal performance.
The experimental setup may be limited by the availability of computational resources and the size of the models being tested.
